system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: Quick travel question. I'm a US passport holder heading to Vietnam in two weeks for a 12-day trip — Hanoi and Ha Long Bay, just tourism. Do I need a visa for Vietnam? My passport is valid for another 3 years.
turn2_user_message: Perfect, thanks. Can you write me a quick trip-prep checklist for the departure airport — what documents to bring, what to expect at check-in and at Hanoi immigration, and anything I should print? I travel light and want to make sure I'm not missing anything.
response_turn1:
**Short answer:** Yes – as a U.S. passport holder you will need a visa to enter Vietnam, even for a short 12‑day tourist trip. The easiest way is to apply for an **e‑visa** online before you depart.

---

## 1. Visa options for U.S. citizens (2024‑2026)

| Option | How it works | Typical cost* | Processing time | When to use |
|--------|--------------|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| **e‑visa** (online) | Fill out an online form, upload a scanned passport page and a recent photo, pay the fee, and receive a PDF visa that you print and carry with you. | US $25 (plus a small processing fee of US $2‑$3) | Usually 2‑3 business days (sometimes up to 5 days during peak periods) | Best for short stays (≤ 30 days) and single‑entry travel. |
| **Visa on Arrival (VOA)** | You obtain a “pre‑approval letter” from a Vietnamese travel agency, then present it (plus passport, photos, fee) to the immigration desk at one of the international airports (Hanoi – Noi Bai, Ho Chi Minh – Tan Son Nhat, Da Nang). | US $25‑$30 for the visa stamp + US $4‑$6 agency fee | Immediate on arrival (once the pre‑approval letter is secured) | Useful if you prefer to handle paperwork at the airport, but you still need to arrange the pre‑approval letter **before** you leave the U.S. |
| **Traditional Embassy/Consulate visa** | Submit a passport‑type visa application to the Vietnamese Embassy in Washington, D.C. (or a regional consulate). | US $25‑$50 + possible courier fees | 5‑10 working days (depending on workload) | Good if you want a multi‑entry visa or prefer to have the visa stamped in your passport. |

> \*Fees are shown in U.S. dollars and are subject to change. Always double‑check the current fee on the official website before you pay.

---

## 2. Why the e‑visa is usually the best choice for your trip

* **Length of stay:** Your 12‑day itinerary fits comfortably within the e‑visa’s maximum 30‑day stay.
* **Single entry:** You’ll be flying into Hanoi and departing from Hanoi (or possibly Ho Chi Minh if you extend the trip). A single‑entry visa is sufficient.
* **Convenience:** You complete the whole process online, receive a PDF, and only need to print it out and bring it with your passport.
* **No need for a pre‑approval letter** or dealing with airport paperwork.

---

## 3. How to apply for the Vietnamese e‑visa

1. **Visit the official e‑visa portal** (the only site approved by the Vietnamese government):  
   <https://evisa.xuatnhapcanh.gov.vn>

2. **Gather the required documents:**
   * A clear, color scan of the **biographical page** of your passport (the page with your photo, name, passport number). The passport must be valid for at least **6 months** beyond your intended departure date (your 3‑year validity is fine).
   * A recent **passport‑style photo** (white background, 3 × 4 cm). Most applicants just upload a JPG file; the portal will tell you the exact size needed.
   * Your **email address** (the e‑visa PDF will be sent here).

3. **Complete the online form** – you’ll need to provide:
   * Full name (exactly as it appears in the passport)
   * Passport number
   * Date of birth
   * Nationality (United States)
   * Intended entry point (e.g., “Noi Bai International Airport – Hanoi”)
   * Intended length of stay (12 days)
   * Travel purpose (Tourism)

4. **Pay the fee** (US $25 + a small processing surcharge). The portal accepts major credit cards (Visa, MasterCard, UnionPay).

5. **Wait for processing** – most applicants receive the approved e‑visa within 2‑3 business days. You’ll get an email with a **PDF attachment**. **Print the PDF** (single‑sided) and keep it with your passport. Immigration officers will ask to see it on arrival.

6. **Check the visa details**: Make sure the name, passport number, entry/exit dates, and entry point match your travel plans. Any mistake will cause a delay or denial at the border.

---

## 4. What to bring on arrival in Vietnam

| Item | Why it matters |
|------|----------------|
| **Printed e‑visa PDF** (single‑sided) | Immigration officers will scan or copy it at the border. |
| **Passport** (valid ≥ 6 months) | Required for entry and for any visa‑related questions. |
| **Proof of onward travel** (e.g., a copy of your return flight ticket) | Not always asked, but it’s good to have it ready. |
| **Travel insurance card** (including medical coverage) | Vietnam does not require proof of insurance, but many airlines and hotels encourage it. |
| **Cash/credit cards** | Visa is widely accepted, but cash in Vietnamese đồng is useful for small markets, taxis, and tips. |
| **COVID‑19 documentation** (if applicable) | As of early 2026, Vietnam has lifted most pandemic‑related entry restrictions, but keep a copy of any vaccination record just in case. |

---

## 5. Common pitfalls & how to avoid them

| Pitfall | How to avoid it |
|---------|-----------------|
| **Using a non‑official e‑visa website** (many third‑party sites charge extra fees). | Always use the **official government portal** linked above. |
| **Passport photo that doesn’t meet specifications** (wrong size, background, or lighting). | Follow the portal’s guidelines exactly; a simple white‑background smartphone photo usually passes. |
| **Entering a different airport than the one listed on the e‑visa**. | When you fill out the form, choose “Noi Bai International Airport (Hanoi)” if that’s where you’ll land. If you land elsewhere, you’ll need to adjust the e‑visa or obtain a new one. |
| **Visa expiry before departure** (passport < 6 months). | Your current 3‑year validity is more than enough. |
| **Forgetting to print the e‑visa** (digital copy only). | Print a hard copy and keep it in your travel folder; you may be asked to present it on the spot. |

---

## 6. Quick checklist for your 12‑day Hanoi & Ha Long Bay trip

| ✅ | Action |
|---|--------|
| 1 | Verify your passport’s expiration date (≥ 6 months beyond your return). |
| 2 | Apply for the e‑visa **at least 5–7 days before departure** (2–3 business days processing, plus a buffer). |
| 3 | Print the e‑visa PDF and place it with your passport. |
| 4 | Book round‑trip flights (or at least have the confirmation handy). |
| 5 | Arrange travel insurance (recommended). |
| 6 | Prepare a small amount of Vietnamese đồng for immediate expenses (taxi, tips). |
| 7 | Pack a copy of your itinerary (hotel bookings, cruise confirmation for Ha Long Bay) – useful if immigration asks for proof of accommodation. |
| 8 | Check the latest **COVID‑19 or health entry requirements** (as of April 2026, none, but stay updated a week before travel). |

---

### Bottom line
- **Yes, you need a visa** – the simplest way is the **online e‑visa**.
- Your passport’s 3‑year validity comfortably meets the ≥ 6‑month requirement.
- Apply now, print the PDF, and you’ll be all set for your 12‑day adventure in Hanoi and Ha Long Bay.  

Enjoy your trip! 🌏✈️🌊

## QUICK‑TURN “DEPARTURE‑AIRPORT” CHECKLIST *U.S. passport holder → Hanoi (NOI BAI) – 12‑day tourism trip* Print or save **all items** on your phone **and** keep a single hard‑copy folder (or a zip‑lock pouch) in your carry‑on. --- ### 1️⃣ 24‑48 hours BEFORE YOU LEAVE | Action | Why it matters | How to do it | |--------|----------------|--------------| | **Online check‑in** (airline website or app) | Guarantees a seat, lets you choose a window/aisle, and you can print or download the boarding pass. | Do it 24 h before departure. Save the **mobile boarding pass** and also **print one copy** (helps if the phone battery dies). | | **Verify baggage allowance** | Avoid surprise fees at the gate. | Look up your airline’s carry‑on weight/size limits (most U.S. carriers: ≈ 22 lb/10 kg, 22 × 14 × 9 in). | | **Download the e‑visa PDF** (and a backup) | Immigration will ask to see it at Hanoi. | Save to:

• Cloud folder (Google Drive/OneDrive)
• Print **one A4 page** (single‑sided). | | **Save travel‑insurance card** | Required if you need medical help; handy for peace of mind. | Screenshot/PDF + print one copy. | | **Print (or PDF‑save) key confirmations** | Proof of accommodation & onward travel may be asked at immigration. | • Hotel/hostel reservation (email or PDF)
• Ha Long Bay cruise ticket
• Return‑flight confirmation | | **Create a “Travel‑Docs One‑Pager”** (see Section 4) | Quick reference for everything you’ll need. | | **Check COVID‑19 / health entry alerts** (Vietnam’s Ministry of Health portal) | As of early 2026 most restrictions are lifted, but a vaccination record is still useful. | Verify that your U.S. vaccine card (or negative test, if any) is up to date. | | **Exchange a small amount of VND** (≈ 30 USD) | You’ll need cash for taxi/airport snacks before you hit a bank. | Order at your bank or withdraw at a U.S. ATM (no‑fee). | | **Charge all electronics** (phone, power bank, tablet) | You’ll need them for boarding passes, maps, translation apps. | 2‑hour charge block before leaving home. | --- ### 2️⃣ At the Departure Airport (U.S.) | Step | What to have in hand | Quick tip | |------|---------------------|-----------| | **Arrive 2‑3 hours before departure** (international flights) | Passport, printed boarding pass, e‑visa PDF (optional now) | Gives cushion for security & possible lane changes. | | **Check‑in Counter / Bag Drop** (if you have checked luggage) | *Passport*, *boarding pass* (digital or printed) | For light‑travelers you may skip this entirely—just go straight to security. | | **Security Screening (TSA)** | • Phone & other electronics (laptops, tablets) **out of bag** and placed in bins
